worth_it_explanation: First-party data is the foundation of audience targeting and conversion measurement in The Trade Desk. Without it, campaigns run on third-party data alone, which carries lower match confidence and no connection to the advertiser's actual customer records. The onboarding check is configuration work, not a rebuild, and the match-rate check costs nothing beyond reading a number in the platform. Catching a low match rate before a campaign launches avoids a situation where a CRM list nominally exists in the account but resolves to too few addressable users to matter.
intro: First-party data onboarding in The Trade Desk connects an advertiser's CRM records and pixel-derived audiences to Unified ID 2.0 (UID2) so those audiences can power targeting and measurement in the Kokai platform. The process hashes personally identifiable information (PII) such as email addresses, sends the hashed values to a UID2 operator, and returns resolved identifiers that The Trade Desk can match against its inventory supply. These checks confirm that the data is actually onboarded and matched to identity, that the match rate is high enough for the data to be actionable, and that the segments are refreshed so they reflect current customers rather than a stale snapshot.

audit_checks.title: CRM or pixel-derived data is not onboarded to UID2
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Without onboarded first-party data, campaigns cannot target the advertiser's own customers or suppress them from acquisition efforts, and conversion measurement has no first-party signal to fall back on when the Universal Pixel drops cookies. The account runs entirely on third-party data, which carries a lower match rate and no connection to known customers.

educate.base:
The Trade Desk uses Unified ID 2.0 (UID2) as its primary identity framework for matching first-party advertiser data to addressable inventory across the open internet. When an advertiser onboards CRM data, such as a list of customer email addresses, the process hashes each address and sends those hashed values to a UID2 operator. The operator resolves the hashes to UID2 identifiers that The Trade Desk can then match against users bidding in the platform. The same process applies to audiences built from the Universal Pixel.

An account that has not completed this onboarding lacks any first-party identity layer. Targeting relies entirely on third-party data from The Trade Desk Data Marketplace, which has no direct relationship to the advertiser's own customer records. Suppression of existing customers from prospecting campaigns is also unavailable without an onboarded first-party list.

The UID2 advertiser and data provider integration guide describes the two paths advertisers use: a direct API integration where the advertiser's systems hash PII and send it to a UID2 operator, and a partner-mediated path where a data platform or clean room handles the hashing and transmission on the advertiser's behalf.

A few signs point to first-party data not being onboarded:
  • The Data Management section of the account shows no first-party audience segments
  • Audience targeting in active ad groups draws only from third-party Data Marketplace providers
  • No Universal Pixel data is flowing into audiences in the platform
You may want to confirm whether the account is using a direct integration, a partner, or a clean room. Each path has its own setup requirements.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer, CRM onboarding typically includes a customer purchase list for loyalty-based targeting and a suppression list to exclude existing buyers from prospecting campaigns. You may want to confirm both lists are onboarded. A purchase list alone without a suppression segment still wastes prospecting spend on customers who already converted.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser, onboarding often starts with a list of past leads to suppress from acquisition campaigns and a list of converted customers to build lookalike audiences against. You may want to confirm the conversion list is available and refreshed. Lookalike targeting degrades when the seed list ages.

learn_more.source_title: Advertiser and data provider integration overview

investigate.intro: Confirm whether the account has first-party audience segments onboarded and whether those segments are wired to any active ad groups.

the_trade_desk_ui.label: The Trade Desk UI
steps: Open 'Data' in the left navigation and select 'My Data'. Look for any first-party audience segments. An empty list, or a list containing only third-party Data Marketplace segments, indicates no first-party onboarding.

For each active campaign, open the ad group targeting and check the audience segments applied. Note whether any first-party segments appear, or whether all audience targeting references Data Marketplace providers only.

Open 'Insights' and check whether the Universal Pixel is recording any data. A pixel with no data, or one that has not been configured to build audiences, is a sign that pixel-derived first-party data is not feeding into targeting.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether any first-party audience segments appear in 'My Data', and whether any active ad groups apply those segments to targeting. An account with at least one onboarded first-party segment that is applied to at least one active ad group passes. An account with no first-party segments, or with segments that are onboarded but not used in any active targeting, is a fail recorded with the segment count and the campaigns affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether first-party segments exist in 'My Data' and whether any active ad groups apply them, then review the evaluation above.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s The Trade Desk account has no first-party audience data onboarded to Unified ID 2.0. Without this, campaigns cannot target the advertiser's own customers, cannot suppress existing buyers from prospecting, and have no first-party identity signal to fall back on when cookie-based measurement drops conversions. {{voice_recommend}} completing the UID2 onboarding process for at least the core CRM lists, either through a direct API integration or a partner, and confirming that the resulting first-party segments appear in 'My Data' and are applied to the relevant ad groups.

audit_checks.title: First-party data match rate is too low for the segments to be actionable
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A match rate below a usable threshold means the onboarded list resolves to too few addressable users to drive meaningful reach or suppression. The segment nominally exists in the account but does not reflect the size of the underlying customer list, so targeting and measurement both work from a fraction of the audience the advertiser expects.

educate.base:
When an advertiser uploads a CRM list, the platform hashes the PII and matches those hashes against UID2 identifiers in the supply it can reach. The resulting segment size reflects only the portion of the uploaded list that resolves to an addressable identifier. The remainder does not resolve. The email may have no associated UID2, the record may have been formatted inconsistently, or the consent signal required for matching may have been absent.

A match rate that is too low means the working audience is a fraction of the CRM list. A list uploaded as 100,000 records that resolves to 3,000 addressable users is not a suppression or targeting instrument at any meaningful scale. The account shows a segment, but the segment does not perform the function the advertiser expects.

Several factors pull match rates down. Inconsistent email formatting, such as a mix of lowercased and mixed-case addresses, produces different hashes for the same address. Records tied to email domains that users rarely access on connected devices, such as work domains, match less reliably than consumer email domains. Consent requirements in European jurisdictions under European Unified ID (EUID) reduce the matchable pool in those regions further.

The UID2 FAQ documentation covers how opt-outs affect the addressable pool, which is the one match-rate reduction the advertiser cannot control. Formatting and consent management are the two areas where the advertiser can improve the number.

A few signs point to a low match rate being the issue:
  • The resolved segment size in the platform is much smaller than the uploaded list
  • The segment has been in the account for a long time and the ratio of resolved to uploaded has not improved
  • The uploaded records include a mix of formatting or a high proportion of business email addresses
You may want to confirm that the upload file normalizes all email addresses to lowercase before hashing. This is the single formatting step with the most consistent impact on match rates.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer, a low match rate on a purchase list limits suppression effectiveness and reduces the quality of lookalike seeds. You may want to check whether the upload file includes email addresses collected at checkout, which carry higher consumer-domain rates and hash more consistently than addresses gathered from promotional signups.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser, a low match rate on a converted-lead list means the lookalike audience seed is smaller than the actual conversion volume would suggest. You may want to confirm the upload file uses the same email format the lead form captured rather than a CRM-exported variant that may have been altered downstream.

learn_more.source_title: Frequently asked questions for advertisers and data providers

investigate.intro: Compare the uploaded list size to the resolved segment size in the platform, and check the upload file for formatting issues that reduce match rates.

the_trade_desk_ui.label: The Trade Desk UI
steps: Open 'Data', then 'My Data', and select a first-party audience segment. Note the resolved size shown in the platform alongside the number of records that were uploaded.
Calculate the rough match rate as resolved size divided by uploaded records. A rate below 20-30% warrants investigation into the upload file's formatting and the domains present in the list.

If the platform surfaces any upload error counts or unmatched record counts, record those figures. A high unmatched count alongside a consistent formatting issue is the most common correctable cause of a low match rate.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the uploaded size and the resolved size for each first-party segment, and calculates the match rate. A segment with a match rate at or above roughly 30% and a resolved size large enough to be usable for its intended purpose (targeting or suppression) passes. A segment with a match rate below roughly 20%, or a resolved size too small to cover its intended audience meaningfully, is a fail recorded with the segment name, the match rate, and any formatting issue identified in the upload file.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the uploaded size and the resolved size for each first-party segment above to calculate the match rate and evaluate it.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more first-party audience segments in {{site_name}}'s The Trade Desk account have a match rate low enough that the segments are not actionable at the expected scale. The uploaded list resolves to a fraction of the original records, so targeting and suppression both work from a much smaller audience than the CRM data would support. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the upload file for email formatting consistency, normalizing all addresses to lowercase before hashing, and re-uploading the corrected list to compare the resolved segment size. If the match rate remains low after formatting corrections, the domain mix in the list, such as a high proportion of business email addresses or records from regions with EUID consent requirements, may be limiting the addressable pool in ways that are harder to correct.

audit_checks.title: First-party audience segments are not refreshed on a regular cadence
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A segment that was accurate at upload decays as customers churn, new customers are added, and UID2 tokens for existing members expire. Suppression lists grow stale and allow recently converted customers back into prospecting. Targeting lists lose coverage of customers who were added to the CRM after the last upload.

educate.base:
A CRM segment uploaded to The Trade Desk reflects the state of the advertiser's customer list at the moment of upload. After that point, the segment diverges from reality in two directions. New customers added to the CRM after the upload are absent from the segment, so they cannot be targeted or suppressed by it. Customers who have churned, or whose records no longer match a valid UID2, remain in the segment until it is refreshed.

UID2 tokens have a limited validity period. When a token expires and the underlying identifier changes, records in a static upload gradually become unresolvable. The segment does not shrink visibly in the platform, but the actual addressable pool within it narrows over time. The UID2 documentation covers token refresh mechanics for operators; for advertisers using uploaded lists rather than a live API integration, re-uploading the list is the equivalent of a token refresh.

The practical effect is different depending on how the segment is used. A suppression list that is never refreshed stops excluding new converters. A lookalike seed that is never refreshed generates audiences modeled against past customers rather than current ones. A retargeting segment that is never refreshed may accumulate records for users who have not been customers for months.

A few signs point to stale segments:
  • The last-updated date on the segment in 'My Data' is many months old despite active customer acquisition
  • The segment predates a significant change in the customer base, such as a product launch or a seasonal sales period
  • The account has no automated pipeline refreshing the list; uploads have been manual and infrequent
You may want to confirm how the segment is being maintained. A partner or clean-room integration may handle refresh automatically while a manual upload process depends on someone remembering to run it.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retailer with ongoing purchases, a suppression list that is not refreshed weekly or biweekly will include recent buyers in prospecting campaigns for a meaningful window. You may want to confirm the suppression segment refresh cadence relative to the purchase rate. A high-volume store accumulates new converters quickly.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser, a converted-lead suppression list that is not refreshed means recently closed leads continue to see acquisition ads. You may want to confirm how quickly the CRM export feeds back into the uploaded segment after a lead is marked as converted.

investigate.intro: Check the last-updated date on each first-party segment and confirm whether a refresh process exists.

the_trade_desk_ui.label: The Trade Desk UI
steps: Open 'Data', then 'My Data', and note the last-updated date displayed for each first-party audience segment.

For any segment with a last-updated date more than 30 days old, ask the account team or the data owner whether the segment is being maintained on a regular schedule or whether it was uploaded once and not revisited.
If the account uses a partner or clean room to manage the upload process, confirm with that partner whether automated refresh is configured and at what cadence it runs.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the last-updated date for each first-party segment and the refresh cadence, if any exists. A segment with a documented refresh process running at least monthly, or a segment maintained through an automated partner integration, passes. A segment with a last-updated date more than 30 days old and no documented refresh process is a fail recorded with the segment name, the last-updated date, and whether the segment is used for suppression, targeting, or lookalike seeding.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the last-updated date and the refresh process, if any, for each first-party segment above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more first-party audience segments in {{site_name}}'s The Trade Desk account have not been refreshed on a regular cadence. Stale segments diverge from the current customer list as new customers are acquired and past customers churn, so suppression coverage degrades and targeting draws on an outdated picture of the audience. {{voice_recommend}} establishing a refresh schedule aligned to the account's customer acquisition rate, with suppression lists refreshed at least monthly and more frequently for accounts with high purchase or conversion volume. If uploads are currently manual, you may want to consider a partner or clean-room integration that handles refresh automatically.
